# Prior work — who has already documented this, and how well

<!-- GENERATED by scripts/make_prior_work.py from findings/prior_work.json + findings/prior_work_sources.json. Do not edit by hand. -->

This survey's claim — that a model slug behind a multi-provider router is not a fixed artifact, so unpinned routing can corrupt a research result — is not novel, and the file exists to say exactly how much of it was already established and by whom.

**16 sources verified, 12 of them new to this repo. 10 further leads are recorded but unchecked.**

Every quotation below was located verbatim in the source itself: abstracts come from the arXiv API, web quotes were found in the live page text, and both are pinned in `findings/prior_work_sources.json` with surrounding context. Titles, authors and dates for papers are fetched, never typed. Items in *Unchecked leads* were surfaced by a search agent and confirmed by nobody — they carry no quotation and must not be cited as evidence until someone verifies them.

`Supports` maps each source to the mistake codes in [`findings/taxonomy.md`](../findings/taxonomy.md).

---

## Direct evidence

### [Model Equality Testing: Which Model Is This API Serving?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.20247)

*paper — Irena Gao, Percy Liang, Carlos Guestrin · ICLR 2025 · arXiv:2410.20247 · 2024-10-26, rev. 2025-04-08*

> 11 out of 31 endpoints serve different distributions than reference weights released by Meta

The closest thing to a peer-reviewed statement of this survey's thesis: an API endpoint's advertised model name is not evidence of which weights it serves. Formalises the question as a two-sample test (MMD with a string kernel) that a caller can actually run, which makes it the natural rigorous upgrade path for anyone who cannot pin a provider.

`Supports: M1, M6, M11`

### [Open weight LLMs exhibit inconsistent performance across providers](https://simonwillison.net/2025/Aug/15/inconsistent-performance/)

*post — Simon Willison (reporting Artificial Analysis data) · simonwillison.net · 2025-08-15*

> with hosted models even knowing the quantization they are using isn’t necessarily enough information to be able to predict that model’s performance

The best single quantitative artifact of same-slug cross-provider divergence, and it is measured on a model OpenRouter serves. On AIME25 (32 runs, reasoning effort high), identical gpt-oss-120b weights scored 93.3% via Cerebras/Nebius/Fireworks/DeepInfra/Novita/Together, 86.7% via Groq, 80.0% via Azure and 36.7% via CompactifAI — a 56.6-point spread under one model name. Willison's note that most 93.3% providers were on the latest vLLM ties the spread to the serving stack, not just precision. Groq and Azure had moved to 93.3% within five days, so the measurement also depends on when it was taken.

`Supports: M1, M3, M8, M11`

### [R1 CoT illegibility revisited](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/jHnZzicKzczkCCArK/r1-cot-illegibility-revisited) *(already cited in this repo)*

*post — nostalgebraist · LessWrong · 2026-04-19*

> I think this is pretty convincing re your original argument that my results were contaminated by bad inference setups, and that correspondingly my paper doesn't substantiate some of its claims and conclusions with good evidence
>
> — Jozdien (Arun Jose), author of the critiqued paper, in the comments

The flagship case, and stronger than this repo previously recorded. Re-running the same GPQA/CoT-legibility pipeline over the same deepseek/deepseek-r1 slug with only the OpenRouter provider changed moved mean illegibility from 4.30 (Targon) to 2.30 (Novita). What this repo had not recorded is the resolution: the author of the critiqued paper conceded in the comments, adding that he "should have been running capability evaluations with different providers" rather than trusting OpenRouter's labels. A published AI-safety result withdrawn in substance because of provider routing, with the author agreeing.

`Supports: M1, M3, M8, M11`

### [Chasing Shadows: Pitfalls in LLM Security Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.09549) *(already cited in this repo)*

*paper — Jonathan Evertz, Niklas Risse, Nicolai Neuer et al. · preprint · arXiv:2512.09549 · 2025-12-10, rev. 2025-12-15*

> every paper contains at least one pitfall, and each pitfall appears in multiple papers. Yet only 15.7% of the present pitfalls were explicitly discussed

Already cited, but under-used: its pitfall P9 (Model Ambiguity) is this survey's thesis, and it is the single most prevalent pitfall in its sample — Present in 73.6% (53/72) of papers and Partly present in a further 12.5%, with none of them acknowledging it (paper section V, P9). Its Table II measures the consequence directly: CodeLlama-7b attack success rises from 18.21% unquantized to 69.52% at 2-bit, and two nominally 4-bit builds of the same model differ 15.49% vs 58.16%. The 73.6%/0-discussed split is an independent echo of this survey's own at-risk-versus-aware result.

`Supports: M1, M6, M10`

### [The Silent Hyperparameter: Quantifying the Impact of Inference Backends on LLM Reproducibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.19537) *(already cited in this repo)*

*paper — David Pape, Jonathan Evertz, Lea Schönherr · preprint · arXiv:2605.19537 · 2026-05-19, rev. 2026-05-20*

> the choice of backend alone can shift benchmark scores by up to 16.6 percentage points and induce high rates of output disagreement

Already cited in reports/openrouter-best-practices.md. Establishes the inference backend as an unreported hyperparameter, with benchmark scores moving by up to 16.6 points on backend choice alone, and finds the stack is almost never reported across the ML literature.

`Supports: M3, M10, M11`

### [Understanding and Mitigating Numerical Sources of Nondeterminism in LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.09501)

*paper — Jiayi Yuan, Hao Li, Xinheng Ding et al. · preprint · arXiv:2506.09501 · 2025-06-11, rev. 2025-10-24*

> can exhibit up to 9% variation in accuracy and 9,000 tokens difference in response length due to differences in GPU count, type, and evaluation batch size

The mechanism underneath the whole thesis: identical weights and greedy decoding still move eval scores when the serving stack changes, because floating-point addition is not associative. This is the peer-reviewable stand-in for the batch-invariance argument, and it means a provider switch can corrupt results even with quantization held constant.

`Supports: M3, M7, M11`

### [When Is the Same Model Not the Same Service? A Measurement Study of Hosted Open-Weight LLM APIs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.02821)

*paper — Haorui Li, Zhenghui He, Xuanzi Liu et al. · preprint · arXiv:2605.02821 · 2026-05-04, rev. 2026-05-07*

> the operational unit is a service object: a provider-specific, time-varying endpoint defined by model variant, protocol behavior, context capacity, listed price, latency and throughput distribution, reliability, and task feasibility

Measurement study of hosted open-weight APIs arguing the operational unit is a provider-specific, time-varying endpoint — model variant, protocol behaviour, context capacity, latency and reliability all vary by provider and by time, not by model name. That is precisely the object this survey says research code must pin. Very recent and not yet independently corroborated.

`Supports: M1, M3, M6, M11`

### [Auditing Black-Box LLM APIs with a Rank-Based Uniformity Test](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.06975)

*paper — Xiaoyuan Zhu, Yaowen Ye, Tianyi Qiu et al. · preprint · arXiv:2506.06975 · 2025-06-08, rev. 2026-04-09*

> API providers may discreetly serve quantized or fine-tuned variants, which can degrade performance and compromise safety

Companion detection method to Model Equality Testing, aimed at the same failure: providers silently serving quantized or fine-tuned variants behind an unchanged model name. Query-efficient enough to run as a routine pre-flight check.

`Supports: M1, M11`

### [Behavioral Consistency and Transparency Analysis on Large Language Model API Gateways](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.21083)

*paper — Guanjie Lin, Yinxin Wan, Shichao Pei et al. · preprint · arXiv:2604.21083 · 2026-04-22*

> reveal frequent gaps between expected and actual behaviors, including silent model substitutions, degraded memory retention, deviations from announced pricing

The first black-box audit aimed squarely at this repo's object of study — third-party API gateways, not individual providers — and it finds silent model substitution in the wild across ten commercial gateways. Important caveat that cuts both ways: the paper anonymises every platform it audits, so it cannot be cited as evidence about OpenRouter specifically, only about the class of service OpenRouter belongs to. That a measurement paper felt obliged to anonymise its subjects is itself worth noting.

`Supports: M1, M6, M11`

### [Why benchmarking is hard](https://epoch.ai/gradient-updates/why-benchmarking-is-hard)

*post — Florian Brand and Jean-Stanislas Denain · Epoch AI · 2025-12-23*

> The selection of an appropriate provider has the biggest impact on model performance

A benchmarking organisation stating the ranking outright, from experience rather than argument. The same post reports provider-specific defects that map onto this survey's taxonomy: empty or cut-off responses before max_tokens is reached (AtlasCloud, Mancer, Fireworks via OpenRouter), and providers whose real max_tokens is lower than advertised.

`Supports: M2, M3, M8`

### [Performance Benchmarking Methodology (Provider Requirements)](https://artificialanalysis.ai/methodology/performance-benchmarking)

*benchmark-org policy — Artificial Analysis · artificialanalysis.ai · accessed 2026-07-21*

> Serve Artificial Analysis a model, quantization, context length, or endpoint configuration that differs from what is publicly advertised and generally available under the same name

A benchmarking organisation has already written this survey's failure mode into enforceable policy, listing it among prohibited provider behaviours with delisting and public disclosure as the sanctions. Institutional confirmation that endpoint configuration diverging from the advertised name is a known, adversarial-enough risk to need rules — and evidence that the fix is procedural, not merely technical.

`Supports: M1, M6, M11`

### [Provider Variance: Introducing Exacto](https://openrouter.ai/blog/announcements/provider-variance-introducing-exacto/)

*vendor statement — Chris Clark · OpenRouter blog · 2025-10-21*

> vary between providers significantly more than the standard benchmarking would suggest

The router itself conceding the premise, from its own billions-of-calls telemetry rather than an outside benchmark — which upgrades this survey's argument from 'critics say' to 'the vendor says'. Cite with the caveat that the same post, and the March 2026 Auto Exacto follow-up, attribute more tool-call breakage to inference-engine and parser bugs than to precision alone; that is a friendly amendment to the quantization-first framing, not a contradiction of it.

`Supports: M3, M11`

### [Llama 3.1: Same model, different results](https://www.together.ai/blog/llama-31-quality)

*vendor statement — Together AI · together.ai blog · 2024-07-31*

> inference services are not fungible

An inference provider publishing its own precision-tier accuracy deltas and stating that identical weights do not imply an equivalent service, because implementation and quality-testing choices 'can mean the difference of a percentage point or more on a benchmark'. Useful precisely because it is a self-report: the disclosure exists only because this vendor chose to make it, which is the argument for logging what actually served each call rather than trusting a label.

`Supports: M1, M8, M11`

### [Provider Routing](https://openrouter.ai/docs/features/provider-routing) *(already cited in this repo)*

*vendor docs — OpenRouter · OpenRouter docs · accessed 2026-07-21*

> Quantized models may exhibit degraded performance for certain prompts, depending on the method used

Already cited. Primary-source documentation of both the hazard and the knobs — quantizations, require_parameters, allow_fallbacks, order/only — none of which are on by default. The warning is in the vendor's own docs, which removes any defence that the risk was undisclosed.

`Supports: M1, M2, M3, M5`

### [Accuracy is Not All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.09141)

*paper — Abhinav Dutta, Sanjeev Krishnan, Nipun Kwatra et al. · NeurIPS 2024 · arXiv:2407.09141 · 2024-07-12*

> the behavior of compressed models as visible to end-users is often significantly different from the baseline model, even when accuracy is similar

Kills the most common defence of a quantized route — "the benchmark score barely moved." Compressed models flip individual answers in both directions and are markedly worse on free-form generation while aggregate accuracy stays put, so an unchanged headline number is not evidence of an unchanged model. Directly relevant to any router claim that rests on a coarse aggregate metric.

`Supports: M1, M8`

### [Investigating the Impact of Quantization Methods on the Safety and Reliability of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.15799)

*paper — Artyom Kharinaev, Viktor Moskvoretskii, Egor Shvetsov et al. · preprint · arXiv:2502.15799 · 2025-02-18, rev. 2025-06-29*

> Our results show both PTQ and QAT can degrade safety alignment

The safety-eval version of the quantization risk, across 66 quantized variants and six compression methods, with no method uniformly safe. Matters here because safety measurements are exactly what much of the surveyed research reports, and because vendor reassurance about quantization is generally framed around task or tool-call quality, never safety behaviour.

`Supports: M1, M8`

---

## Unchecked leads

Surfaced by search, not verified by anyone here. Listed so the search does not have to be repeated, and so nobody mistakes an unchecked lead for a source.

| Lead | Where | Tier | Why it might matter |
|---|---|---|---|
| [Reflection 70B benchmark dispute (independent testers could not verify what sat behind the API)](https://the-decoder.com/reflection-70b-launch-mired-in-controversy-as-third-party-benchmarks-disappoint/) | VentureBeat / The Decoder / Artificial Analysis 2024-09 | direct | Reported as the textbook case of an opaque endpoint producing a headline score no one could reproduce from the released weights. Would be the strongest incident citation if confirmed against primary sources. |
| [Inspect AI provider docs: OpenRouter routing and pinning guidance](https://inspect.aisi.org.uk/providers.html) | UK AI Safety Institute current | direct | Reported to warn that OpenRouter may split one model's requests across backends and to document the pinning arguments — an eval-harness maintainer converging independently on this survey's advice. Being audited separately against the taxonomy; see reports/provider-ab-experiment-plan.md. |
| [RooCodeInc/Roo-Code #11325: default OpenRouter routing to FP4/Int4 providers corrupts CJK output](https://github.com/RooCodeInc/Roo-Code/issues/11325) | GitHub 2026-02 | direct | Reported user-visible corruption (raw Unicode escapes instead of Korean/Chinese/Japanese text) tied to low-bit providers on the same slug. Single-report and not independently replicated, but a concrete non-accuracy failure mode. |
| [Llama 4 Maverick: experimental variant ranked #2 on LMArena, public release ranked far lower; LMArena policy updated in response](https://arena.ai/blog/policy/) | TechCrunch / The Register / LMArena policy 2025-04 | adjacent | Reported as a leaderboard-level analogue: the benchmarked artifact was not the shipped artifact. LMArena's resulting written policy reportedly requires providers to confirm the tested model is identical to the released one, with removal as the sanction. |
| [Open LLM Leaderboard: local-inference-only policy with pinned model revisions](https://huggingface.co/docs/leaderboards/en/open_llm_leaderboard/about) | Hugging Face current | adjacent | Reported to restrict the leaderboard to open weights because hosted APIs can change unpredictably, and to re-run everything locally with a pinned revision — a leaderboard sidestepping the problem structurally rather than pinning a provider. |
| [HELM: a model can have more than one deployment, named by host organisation](https://github.com/stanford-crfm/helm/blob/main/docs/adding_new_models.md) | Stanford CRFM current | adjacent | Reported to encode provider identity into the model's canonical name (together/gemma-2-9b-it vs google/gemma-2-9b-it). Unconfirmed whether run results record which deployment served them, which is the part that would matter for M4. |
| [block/goose #3846: OpenRouter defaults to a quantization tier users cannot control per provider](https://github.com/block/goose/issues/3846) | GitHub 2025-08 | adjacent | Reported downstream-tool complaint in the same family as qwen-code PR #348: the safe routing knobs exist but are not reachable where users need them. |
| [QwenLM/qwen-code PR #348: rejected default to avoid quantized OpenRouter providers](https://github.com/QwenLM/qwen-code/pull/348) | GitHub 2025-08-15 | adjacent | Already cited in reports/openrouter-best-practices.md as evidence that no one upstream will pin routing for you. Carried here for completeness; the claim of 'substantially worse output for coding' is a developer report with no measurement attached. |
| [inference-engine-bugs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.09713) | arXiv:2506.09713 ACM TOSEM | adjacent | Reported empirical study of 929 bugs across five inference engines, with a large share presenting as silent wrong output rather than crashes. Supports the engine-level half of provider variance. |
| [commercial-llm-reproducibility-se](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.25506) | arXiv:2510.25506 ICSE 2026 | adjacent | Reported that of 18 artifact-bearing papers using commercial LLM APIs, only 5 were executable and none fully reproduced — the closed-API analogue of this survey, in software engineering. |
